I have been thinking about this photo a lot.

It's a beautiful testament to human civilization and our ability to reach for the stars.

But the thing that I can't get over is that a *human being* took this photo. From a tin cylinder flying on the other side of the moon. 

A machine could have taken it and it would have still been cool. But man was there. And that makes it so much more evocative.

items.title: The layer that can route to the best AI model for the particular job is going to…
items.summaryText:
The layer that can route to the best AI model for the particular job is going to increase in value substantially. There are at least 3 big reasons: 

* Cost optimization: there are plenty of use cases where you need frontier intelligence for some tasks and something far cheaper for others. Even in the same task you may use frontier intelligence for planning and review of the work, but an OSS or cheaper model for the bulk of the workload. This is going to be standard across large buckets of work going forward. 

* Capability maximization: despite the bitter lesson and models generally getting better in the same direction, there are still lots of differences between models. Some are better at tool use, others better at coding, and others again better at certain domains of knowledge work. The ability to route between these at different times is a huge advantage.

* Risk mitigation: while the Fable situation is somewhat of a black swan, it’s possible we’re heading toward a regulatory environment where governments may restrict models at different times based on their approval mechanisms or new things they discover. This means you’re going to want flexibility in being able to deploy workloads across different providers as a form of risk mitigation.

Ultimately, it’s going to increasingly be a a strategic advantage for the applied AI layer that they can effectively route between models. Will be very interesting to see how this evolves.

metadata.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

items.summaryZh:
能够针对特定任务路由到最佳AI模型的层将大幅增值。至少有三大原因：

* 成本优化：很多用例中，某些任务需要前沿智能，而其他任务则可以用更便宜的模型。即使是同一任务，也可能在前沿智能用于规划和工作审查，而用开源或更便宜的模型处理大部分工作量。这将成为未来大量工作的标准。

* 能力最大化：尽管有苦涩的教训，模型总体上朝同一方向改进，但模型间仍有很大差异。有些更擅长工具使用，有些更擅长编码，还有一些更擅长某些知识工作领域。在不同时间在这些模型间路由的能力是一个巨大优势。

* 风险缓解：虽然Fable的情况有点像黑天鹅事件，但我们可能正走向一个监管环境，政府可能会根据其审批机制或新发现的不同时间限制模型。这意味着你希望有能力在不同提供商之间部署工作量，作为风险缓解的一种形式。

最终，能够有效在模型间路由将日益成为应用AI层的战略优势。看其如何演变将会非常有趣。

items.title: Having been through many frontier model launch reviews, I have empathy for every…
items.summaryText:
Having been through many frontier model launch reviews, I have empathy for everyone involved.

Launching an LLM isn't like shipping traditional software - you're making a decision about a black box with effectively infinite use cases and infinite failure modes.

The tradeoffs are hard - every increase in capability expands the space of both valuable use cases and potential misuse.

As a lab, you build extensive evals, you red-team, you iterate on the model. You debate tradeoffs across candidate checkpoints before choosing the best one to launch.

Then early-access partners still uncover behaviors you didn't anticipate.

You can never be 100% certain you've understood a frontier model. You focus on reducing the uncertainty enough to launch.

As frontier models become smarter across the industry, that decision will get harder - for labs and regulators.

metadata.bio: Prev: Product Leader at Google - Gemini, Veo, Nano Banana.

items.summaryZh:
经历过多次前沿模型发布审查，我对所有参与者都感同身受。

发布LLM不像发布传统软件——你是在对一个黑箱做决策，它实际上有无限的用例和无限的失败模式。

权衡很难——每一次能力提升都会扩大有价值用例和潜在滥用的空间。

作为实验室，你要构建广泛的评估，进行红队测试，迭代模型。你在候选检查点之间辩论权衡，然后选择最佳的那个发布。

然后早期接入伙伴仍然会发现你未曾预料的行为。

你永远无法100%确定自己理解了一个前沿模型。你专注于将不确定性降低到足以发布的程度。

随着行业前沿模型变得越来越智能，这个决策将变得更加困难——对实验室和监管者而言。

items.title: This whole Fable export control situation is actually net positive to regulation…
items.summaryText:
This whole Fable export control situation is actually net positive to regulation discourse. It’s an early peek into what AI regulation would end up looking like at scale when enacted at the model layer instead of the specific application of the AI.

The government would have sole discretion over when a model can be released to the to public, based on a bunch of factors that they inherently control. 

In this case, based on the available reporting, the risk is that the model can be jailbroken to deliver increased cyber exploit capabilities. The issue is that actually you want models to be able to have those capabilities on the defense side of cyber as well, and for all intents and purposes, by Anthropic’s own response, you can execute these capabilities today in other models. 

So thus the whole challenge will be that you’re debating with the government, over months and months, with every model release, what these models are actually capable of and what their risks are. Inherently, there’s not only a lot of subjectivity in determining those risks, but there’s also many other factors that go into the risks being practical in the first place.

The net result is that we would end up with backlog of AI releases, progress in the market inherently would dramatically slow down, and AI would start to look more like any other sclerotic industry. If this paradigm had existed 3 years ago at the start of the current AI wave, we’d likely currently be stuck on GPT-4 level intelligence at this point.

This is why, wherever possible, we should be regulating the applied use of AI. We should continue to study and enforce the dangerous use of AI in cyber attacks, financial services risks, fraud, biowarfare, and other spaces. AI safety is incredibly important, but slowing down progress this early in the development of AI I suspect is net harmful.

metadata.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

items.summaryZh:
整个Fable出口管制情况实际上对监管讨论是净正面。它让我们提前窥见，当AI监管在模型层面而非具体应用层面实施时，大规模监管会是什么样子。

政府将根据他们自身控制的诸多因素，独自决定模型何时可以向公众发布。

在这种情况下，根据现有报道，风险在于模型可能被越狱，从而增强网络攻击能力。问题在于，实际上你也希望模型在网络安全防御方面具备这些能力，而且从Anthropic自己的回应来看，今天在其他模型中已经可以执行这些能力。

因此，整个挑战在于，你将与政府就每个模型发布进行数月之久的争论，讨论这些模型的实际能力和风险。本质上，不仅确定这些风险存在大量主观性，而且还有许多其他因素首先使得这些风险变得实际可行。

最终结果是，我们将面临AI发布的积压，市场进展将显著放缓，AI将开始看起来像任何其他僵化的行业。如果这种模式三年前在当前AI浪潮开始时存在，我们现在可能还停留在GPT-4级别的智能。

这就是为什么，在可能的情况下，我们应该监管AI的应用使用。我们应该继续研究并执行对AI在网络攻击、金融服务风险、欺诈、生物战等领域的危险使用的监管。AI安全非常重要，但我认为在AI发展的早期阶段放慢进展是净有害的。

items.title: Biohub: The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Co-Founders Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Head of Science Alex Rives
items.summaryText:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:02
We just wanna give tools to the whole scientific community.

Speaker 2 | 00:02 - 00:17
We wanna understand how biology works. I wanna understand the genetics of this person. I wanna understand the risks they have to different illnesses. My goal is to be…
items.bodyText:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:02
We just wanna give tools to the whole scientific community.

Speaker 2 | 00:02 - 00:17
We wanna understand how biology works. I wanna understand the genetics of this person. I wanna understand the risks they have to different illnesses. My goal is to be able to treat the individual as an individual, understand the mechanisms, and be able to intervene.

Speaker 1 | 00:17 - 00:34
We'll have a bigger impact by getting us in more scientists hands quicker by doing it as open source projects instead. It's not just like there's some factory somewhere that you can pay to produce the data. You actually need to invent new novel scientific approaches. The theory isn't that we're gonna cure the diseases. We're not.

Speaker 1 | 00:34 - 00:36
It's that we wanna help accelerate the pace of progress for the

Speaker 3 | 00:36 - 00:42
whole scientific field. We folded over 1,100,000,000 proteins and predicted their structures,

Speaker 4 | 00:42 - 00:50
and we didn't design a model for antibodies. We didn't design a model to be able to bind one particular target. We just designed a model that could understand proteins.

Speaker 2 | 00:50 - 00:58
If we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone.

Speaker 5 | 01:05 - 01:18
Today on No Priors, we're joined by Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Reeves. We'll be talking about Biohub and all their various efforts to now start applying AI at scale to do world models of cells and different levels of interactions across biology.

Speaker 4 | 01:19 - 01:21
Mark, Priscilla, thank you for doing this.

Speaker 1 | 01:22 - 01:22
Thanks for having

Speaker 4 | 01:22 - 01:23
It us

Speaker 3 | 01:23 - 01:23
was fun.

Speaker 4 | 01:24 - 01:26
Congratulations on new missions.

Speaker 3 | 01:26 - 01:26
Thank you.

Speaker 4 | 01:26 - 01:41
You guys made Biohub your primary philanthropic effort and then committed $500,000,000 to this virtual biology initiative. Can you tell us a little bit about, you know, why do that and how did you go from we should fund this to this is like

Speaker 2 | 01:41 - 02:04
who we are? So Biohub in its current form, we're super excited about. We feel like it's a really good fit for who we are and what we bring to the table and what we can achieve together. But this work started ten years ago when we were thinking about how can we give back? And wanted to build an organization that could cure, prevent, and manage all disease by the end of the century.

Speaker 2 | 02:05 - 02:15
And we had a series of hilarious meetings with scientists that, like, famous Nobel Prize winning scientists were just laughing at us.

Speaker 4 | 02:15 - 02:17
Was that your starting line? We're just gonna cure all disease?

Speaker 1 | 02:17 - 02:34
No. No. And to be clear, we don't think that we're gonna be the ones curing the diseases. Our goal is always to build tools that could accelerate the whole scientific field, so that way the scientific field collectively could cure all the diseases. But still people thought that by the end of the century it was a stretch.

Speaker 1 | 02:34 - 02:35
Now I think it's like too conservative.

Speaker 2 | 02:36 - 02:56
And so we kept being like, okay, well, we had these series of funny, awkward educational conversations where we were like, okay, but like, why? Like, why do you think it's impossible? And like, you know, just being the person in the room is just like, oh, I don't know why. You tell me. Finally, we got people to like, they're like, fine.

Speaker 2 | 02:56 - 03:08
If you really must know. And we're like, you know, we do. It seems important. You know, they were like, well, we work in silos. And when you publish, information doesn't get shared.

Speaker 2 | 03:08 - 03:32
It gets locked up for long periods of time. And we don't have tooling. You know, they gave the example of like, we build a great tool by one postdoc in a lab and it lives on their computer. And when they graduate, the tool is gone. And we heard was very hard to build shared tools to move science faster, build a shared knowledge base to quickly move science faster.

Speaker 2 | 03:33 - 03:39
And that's sort of where we began in thinking about, okay, like if those are the problems, like what can we contribute?

Speaker 1 | 03:40 - 04:03
Yeah. I mean, so the original Biohub model was basically focus on long term tool development by bringing together engineers and scientists across multiple universities to focus on long term tool development. And basically, it like worked. And, you know, we started off with with CZI doing a number of different things. And I think over time, we just felt like, okay.
